reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper presents a physics-informed neural network model to estimate and predict 3D rotational dynamics from image sequences of freely rotating 3D rigid bodies. The model uses a multi-stage prediction pipeline that maps individual images to a latent representation homeomorphic to SO(3), computes angular velocities from latent pairs, and predicts future latent states using the Hamiltonian equations of motion with a learned representation of the Hamiltonian. The efficacy of the approach is demonstrated on a new rotating rigid-body dataset with sequences of rotating cubes and rectangular prisms with uniform and non-uniform density.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses: The paper presents a novel approach to learning 3D rigid-body dynamics from images using a Hamiltonian-based prior. The strengths of the paper include its technical soundness, clear writing, and well-organized structure. The model outperforms baseline models on most datasets and provides an interpretable latent space. However, the paper could be improved by addressing limitations such as testing in idealized settings and exploring transfer/few-shot learning between different 3D rigid-body systems.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper proposes a novel method called Consolidator for efficient transfer learning in large vision models. Consolidator modifies the pre-trained model by adding a small set of tunable parameters to temporarily store task-specific knowledge while freezing the backbone model during adaptation. The proposed consolidator is designed as a mergeable adapter that accompanies the fully connected (FC) layer in vision models. It adopts grouped connections across the features extracted by FC layers to construct tunable parts in a consolidator. The consolidator can be consolidated into the original FC layer of a vision model, leading to no extra inference cost. The paper evaluates the performance of Consolidator on various downstream recognition tasks and shows that it can reach up to 7.56 better accuracy than full fine-tuning with merely 0.35% parameters per task.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses: Strengths: The proposed consolidator method achieves efficient transfer learning for large vision models. It reduces the storage space by using grouped connected layers and consolidating some duplicate entries. The training time non-linearity, e.g., droppath, effectively enriches model capacity under a constrained storage budget. The consolidator can be easily expanded as a multi-branch topology without breaking the linearity. It outperforms state-of-the-art parameter-efficient tuning methods by a clear margin. The consolidator shows great power on other fundamental visual tasks, i.e., object detection and semantic segmentation. Weaknesses: The paper could benefit from a more detailed comparison with other state-of-the-art methods. The effect of individual components in the module design is not thoroughly investigated. The paper assumes that the cross-channel information exchange is redundant in visual adaptation, which may not always be true. The unstructured consolidator can be a sub-optimal choice when needed, with a slight performance drop and more training cost.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper proposes a new methodology called Dateformer, which utilizes Transformers for long-term time series forecasting. Dateformer splits time series into patches by day and reforms point-wise to patch-wise processing, enhancing information input and output. It also distills the whole training set's global information into time representations, which are used as the main modeling entities for Transformers. Dateformer achieves state-of-the-art performance on 7 real-world datasets with a 33.6% relative improvement and extends the maximum forecast range to half-year.
